option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-matched, affordable first-line for generalized convulsive seizures here, and monotherapy should be optimized (dose/adherence) before adding a second drug.
option_2_rationale: If convulsions recur despite adequate valproate dosing, clobazam is a practical adjunct option that can improve control without switching off the appropriate anchor drug.
option_3_label: Add phenobarbital as the lowest-cost escalation
option_3_rationale: If seizures persist and resources are very limited (or clobazam unavailable), phenobarbital is an accessible adjunct in Uganda, used despite sedation risk, to improve seizure control.

option_1_rationale: Phenytoin matches focal-to-bilateral tonic-clonic seizures and has not been shown to have failed at an adequate dose/adherence yet, so optimize/confirm dosing before changing therapy.
option_2_rationale: Carbamazepine is the default first-line, cost-sensitive focal ASM in this setting and is well-suited for focal seizures with secondary generalization for long-term management.
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (accessibility fallback)
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for focal-to-bilateral tonic-clonic seizures, providing a practical alternative if carbamazepine is unavailable or not tolerated.

option_1_rationale: With seizure type not yet characterized, valproate is the best broad-spectrum, cost-effective anchor in this setting and is appropriate for many childhood generalized/mixed epilepsies.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and affordable in Uganda and is a reasonable first monotherapy when seizure type is uncertain and resources are limited, despite sedation risk.
option_3_label: Start broad-spectrum alternative (if VPA/PB not suitable)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and can be started quickly with fewer interactions, making it a practical alternative if valproate or phenobarbital are not tolerated or feasible.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-matched, cost-practical broad-spectrum ASM for myoclonic-atonic (Doose) seizures, and monotherapy has not yet been optimized.
option_2_label: Valproate plus clobazam (if control remains inadequate after optimizing VPA)
option_2_rationale: If seizures remain frequent despite an adequate valproate trial, clobazam is a practical, accessible adjunct that can help without worsening myoclonic features.
option_3_label: Topiramate monotherapy (if valproate is not tolerated/contraindicated/unavailable)
option_3_rationale: Topiramate is a reasonable broad-spectrum alternative for myoclonic-atonic epilepsy when valproate cannot be used, with the trade-off of potential cognitive side effects.

option_1_label: Switch to valproate monotherapy (preferred anchor for generalized)
option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate low-cost broad-spectrum anchor for generalized tonic seizures here, and CBZ is not well-matched given ongoing frequent seizures.
option_2_label: Valproate + clobazam bridging for rapid control
option_2_rationale: With ~3 seizures/day, adding clobazam can provide faster symptomatic control while valproate is established, using accessible medications and avoiding CBZ mismatch.
option_3_label: Switch to phenytoin monotherapy (tonic-focused fallback)
option_3_rationale: If valproate is unavailable or not tolerated, phenytoin is a pragmatic tonic-seizure-effective alternative, though with greater long-term adverse-effect/interaction burden.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the Uganda-appropriate first-line for focal seizures, and monotherapy has not been documented as failed.
option_2_label: If seizures persist despite adequate CBZ, add an accessible adjunct
option_2_rationale: For breakthrough focal seizures after optimized carbamazepine, clobazam is a practical add-on in this setting while avoiding immediate costly switches.
option_3_label: If CBZ is ineffective or not tolerated, switch to an alternative monotherapy
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is an effective focal-seizure monotherapy option and may be preferable if carbamazepine causes adverse effects or fails at an adequate dose.

option_1_rationale: Seizure type is unspecified, so a broad-spectrum, cost-feasible ASM is safest; valproate is the Uganda baseline for generalized/mixed or uncertain seizure types and avoids the risk of worsening generalized seizures seen with carbamazepine/phenytoin.
option_2_rationale: If valproate is not tolerated/available or if weight/metabolic concerns emerge, lamotrigine is a broad-spectrum alternative with relatively favorable cognitive/behavior profile, acknowledging the need for slow titration.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (rapid-start alternative)
option_3_rationale: If rapid initiation is needed or valproate is unsuitable, levetiracetam provides broad-spectrum coverage with minimal titration, but should be monitored closely for irritability/behavioral worsening in a child with ASD/hyperactivity.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate low-cost broad-spectrum first-line for GTCS here, and ongoing frequent seizures on carbamazepine suggest inadequate control and/or mismatch.
option_2_rationale: If valproate is unavailable or there are hepatic/nutritional concerns, phenobarbital is accessible and effective for GTCS, recognizing sedation risk in a developmentally delayed infant.
option_3_label: Continue carbamazepine and add clobazam (if switching not feasible immediately)
option_3_rationale: A temporary escalation strategy if an immediate switch cannot be done, but it is less ideal than switching to a generalized-appropriate monotherapy and adds sedation/polytherapy burden.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, cost-appropriate broad-spectrum choice for presumed generalized tonic-clonic seizures and is a better match than carbamazepine.
option_2_label: Continue carbamazepine only if seizure semiology is actually focal-to-bilateral (hold strategy while clarifying)
option_2_rationale: If further history/EEG suggests focal onset with secondary generalization (mis-labeled as “generalized”), carbamazepine remains appropriate and should be optimized before switching.
option_3_label: Valproate plus clobazam (if seizures remain very frequent or during transition)
option_3_rationale: If seizure clusters persist or rapid additional control is needed while optimizing valproate, clobazam is an accessible adjunct for short-to-medium term escalation.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the standard first-line, affordable focal-seizure medication in this setting, appropriate for focal motor seizures with impaired awareness.
option_2_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (most accessible infant option)
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for focal seizures in infants, and is a practical choice when rapid seizure control and medication access are major concerns.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (fallback focal agent)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is an accessible focal ASM and reasonable if carbamazepine or phenobarbital are not tolerated or not available, acknowledging its higher toxicity/interaction burden.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate first-line, broad-spectrum, cost-effective option for GTCS in this Uganda setting with no stated contraindications.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for GTCS and is a pragmatic first-line alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (fallback option)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is accessible and can control tonic-clonic seizures, serving as a reasonable fallback if valproate/phenobarbital cannot be used.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-supported, cost-conscious first-line for GTCS here and the patient is currently seizure-free, so switching or adding therapy is not justified.
option_2_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (fallback if valproate not feasible)
option_2_rationale: If valproate cannot be continued (e.g., supply or intolerance), phenobarbital is a widely available, effective, low-cost alternative for generalized seizures in this setting.
option_3_label: Switch to levetiracetam monotherapy (if valproate not tolerated and PB undesirable)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and can be initiated without slow titration, making it a reasonable alternative if valproate is unsuitable and phenobarbital’s sedation is a major concern.

option_1_label: Continue/optimize carbamazepine while clarifying seizure type
option_1_rationale: Monotherapy has not been shown to be optimized or failed; continuing CBZ is reasonable if seizures are focal, but seizure classification should be clarified promptly to avoid CBZ in absence/myoclonic epilepsy.
option_2_rationale: With seizure type not documented, valproate is the most practical broad-spectrum, cost-sensitive anchor in this setting and avoids the risk of CBZ worsening unrecognized generalized seizure types.
option_3_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (if valproate is not suitable or not tolerated)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is broad-spectrum and generally cognition-friendly in a child with developmental delay/autism, but it requires slow titration and is a less cost/availability-favored choice than valproate.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is appropriate for absence and provides broader coverage given the atypically long “10-minute” episodes; monotherapy should be optimized before switching/adding.
option_2_label: Switch to absence-specific monotherapy (if episodes are confirmed pure absence)
option_2_rationale: If assessment/EEG supports pure absence without other seizure types, ethosuximide is a best-fit, cost-reasonable absence agent and avoids longer-term valproate burdens.
option_3_label: Adjunct strategy for persistent seizures after adequate valproate trial
option_3_rationale: If valproate monotherapy has been genuinely optimized and absence seizures persist (or mixed generalized features are suspected), adding lamotrigine is a reasonable broad-spectrum escalation (with slow titration due to rash risk and valproate interaction).

option_1_rationale: Unclassified prolonged convulsive seizures favor a broad-spectrum, cost-effective anchor; with no recent seizures reported and no documented monotherapy failure, continuing/optimizing valproate is preferred.
option_2_label: Add low-cost adjunct for breakthrough prolonged convulsions
option_2_rationale: If prolonged convulsive seizures recur despite adequate valproate dosing, clobazam is an accessible adjunct that can reduce breakthrough events without requiring switching off the effective broad-spectrum anchor.
option_3_label: Pragmatic switch if valproate cannot be used
option_3_rationale: If valproate is not tolerated, unavailable, or contraindicated on further evaluation, phenobarbital is a widely available, low-cost monotherapy option for convulsive seizures in this setting.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, affordable broad-spectrum anchor for generalized convulsive seizures in this setting, and CBZ is a poorer match for true generalized epilepsy.
option_2_rationale: If valproate is unavailable/poorly tolerated, phenobarbital is a widely accessible effective monotherapy for generalized tonic-clonic seizures, accepting sedation risk.
option_3_label: Adjunct approach if switch is not feasible immediately (transition/bridging strategy)
option_3_rationale: If an immediate switch cannot be done (access or clinical caution), adding clobazam can reduce breakthrough convulsions short-term while reassessing seizure classification and planning a switch to a broad-spectrum monotherapy.

option_1_label: Start valproate monotherapy (covers absence + eyelid myoclonia)
option_1_rationale: Broad-spectrum first-line for absence with myoclonic features and practical in Uganda, with good likelihood of controlling both absence and eyelid myoclonia as monotherapy.
option_2_rationale: Highly effective targeted monotherapy for frequent absence seizures if the phenotype is truly absence-only and no other generalized seizure types are present.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (slower-titration alternative)
option_3_rationale: Reasonable alternative for absence-spectrum epilepsy when valproate/ethosuximide are not tolerated or unavailable, acknowledging slower titration and potentially less robust control of myoclonic elements.

option_1_rationale: Focal semiology makes carbamazepine the most appropriate, cost-effective first-line anchor in this setting, with less sedation than phenobarbital.
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is unavailable or not tolerated, phenobarbital is a widely available, low-cost focal-seizure option, accepting sedation risk.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (alternative older focal agent)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is another affordable focal-seizure monotherapy option when carbamazepine cannot be used, though with higher long-term adverse-effect and interaction burden.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best first-line anchor for GTCS in this setting, and monotherapy should be optimized before adding drugs; carbamazepine is not preferred for primary generalized epilepsy.
option_2_label: Valproate + clobazam adjunct for breakthrough/prolonged seizures
option_2_rationale: If seizures persist despite adequate valproate dosing, clobazam is an accessible add-on for improved control and can help in patients with prolonged convulsions.
option_3_label: Valproate + phenobarbital as a low-cost fallback polytherapy
option_3_rationale: If clobazam is unavailable/ineffective or cost constraints dominate, phenobarbital is a widely available alternative adjunct, accepting sedation/cognitive risks and monitoring closely.

option_1_rationale: Broad-spectrum, strong efficacy for generalized convulsive seizures and is the default generalized anchor in this setting when a daily ASM is justified.
option_2_rationale: Highly available/affordable in Uganda and effective for generalized convulsions, reasonable if valproate is avoided or monitoring is limited.
option_3_label: No daily ASM (watchful strategy for febrile seizures)
option_3_rationale: With brief febrile generalized convulsions and no prolonged/status episodes, many children do not require continuous antiseizure prophylaxis; prioritize fever management and reassess if seizures become more frequent/prolonged.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred, cost-effective broad-spectrum first-line ASM for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting and can be optimized as monotherapy before adding other drugs.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for generalized seizures in Uganda and is a practical alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (broad-spectrum alternative)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is an effective broad-spectrum option for GTCS when valproate/phenobarbital are unsuitable, recognizing that it requires slow titration and thus may control seizures less rapidly early on.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the Uganda-appropriate first-line for focal seizures, and there is no clear evidence yet of optimized-dose failure or intolerance.
option_2_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (if carbamazepine not tolerated/ineffective)
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine cannot be used or has failed at an adequate dose, lamotrigine is a focal-effective alternative with a more favorable cognitive profile (though it requires slow titration).
option_3_label: Switch to phenytoin monotherapy (low-cost fallback)
option_3_rationale: If lamotrigine is not feasible/available and carbamazepine is failing or not tolerated, phenytoin is an accessible focal-seizure option in this resource-limited setting.

option_1_rationale: She is seizure-free on carbamazepine, and with no documented adverse effects or breakthrough seizures, maintaining the working monotherapy is the most conservative and practical approach while clarifying the exact generalized seizure subtype.
option_2_label: Switch to valproate monotherapy (if true generalized syndrome suspected)
option_2_rationale: If history/EEG suggests a generalized epilepsy where carbamazepine could worsen absence/myoclonic seizures, valproate is the most effective low-cost broad-spectrum option in this setting.
option_3_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (valproate-avoidant strategy)
option_3_rationale: If you want to avoid valproate in a female child and generalized epilepsy is confirmed, lamotrigine is a broad-spectrum alternative (noting it requires slow titration and may be less ideal if myoclonus is prominent).

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective broad-spectrum, cost-feasible first-line option for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting, and monotherapy is appropriate at Visit 1.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for tonic-clonic seizures and is a practical first-line alternative if valproate is unavailable, unaffordable, or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start carbamazepine monotherapy (fallback tonic-clonic control)
option_3_rationale: Carbamazepine can control tonic-clonic seizures and is commonly available, serving as a reasonable fallback if valproate and phenobarbital are not suitable.

option_1_label: Continue and optimize focal first-line (CBZ) monotherapy
option_1_rationale: Focal seizures with no documented failure—carbamazepine is the Uganda first-line match and should be optimized before any add-on or switch.
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is ineffective or not tolerated, phenobarbital is widely available and effective for focal seizures, recognizing sedation risk in a developmentally impaired child.
option_3_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (less sedating alternative if available)
option_3_rationale: If carbamazepine cannot be used (e.g., adverse effects/drug interactions) and sedation is a major concern, lamotrigine is a reasonable focal option, though it requires slow titration.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the preferred first-line, affordable focal-seizure ASM in this setting and fits the semiology described.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for focal seizures, a reasonable alternative if carbamazepine is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (if CBZ unsuitable or interaction concerns)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is effective for focal epilepsy and is a good alternative when carbamazepine cannot be used, recognizing slower titration and potential cost/availability limits.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the correct first-line drug for focal motor seizures in this setting, and with no documented adequate-dose failure, monotherapy should be optimized before adding/switching.
option_2_label: Add-on therapy for persistent daily seizures (if CBZ optimized but still uncontrolled)
option_2_rationale: If seizures continue despite optimized carbamazepine, clobazam is an accessible adjunct that can reduce breakthrough seizures, with sedation risk to monitor given school difficulties.
option_3_label: Practical switch if carbamazepine is ineffective or not tolerated
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for focal seizures and is a reasonable low-cost fallback when carbamazepine cannot be used, recognizing potential cognitive/sedation effects.

option_1_rationale: Levetiracetam fits focal motor seizures and the child is currently seizure-free, so continuing successful monotherapy is preferred over switching or adding drugs.
option_2_label: Cost-sensitive focal-first switch if LEV access is unreliable
option_2_rationale: Carbamazepine is the Uganda baseline first-line for focal seizures and may be more affordable/available if levetiracetam continuity cannot be ensured.
option_3_label: Fallback affordable focal monotherapy if CBZ not tolerated/available
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for focal seizures in resource-limited settings, used as a practical alternative when other options are not feasible despite sedation risk.

option_1_label: Optimize valproate-first strategy (reduce unnecessary polytherapy if possible)
option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best first-line for pediatric GTCS here; if seizures are controlled or sedation/developmental impact is a concern, simplifying to optimized valproate monotherapy is preferred.
option_2_label: Continue current dual therapy while confirming control and optimizing doses
option_2_rationale: If GTCS are still occurring or prior attempts at monotherapy failed, maintaining this practical, locally available combination while ensuring adequate valproate dosing/adherence is reasonable.
option_3_label: Valproate plus levetiracetam (if clobazam ineffective or too sedating)
option_3_rationale: For persistent seizures or problematic sedation on clobazam, switching the adjunct to levetiracetam provides broad-spectrum GTCS coverage with less benzodiazepine-related cognitive/sedation burden.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred first-line, broad-spectrum, cost-effective anchor for frequent generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting with no stated contraindications.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly accessible and effective for GTCS in Uganda and is a reasonable alternative if valproate cannot be used, with counseling about sedation and school impact.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (non-valproate alternative)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is a broad-spectrum option for GTCS when valproate is not suitable, but requires slow titration and careful rash monitoring.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the Uganda-appropriate first-line for focal seizures with secondary generalization, and there is no clear evidence yet of failure at an adequate dose.
option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct after CBZ optimization (CBZ + CLB)
option_2_rationale: If seizures persist despite optimized carbamazepine, adding clobazam is a practical, mechanistically complementary, accessible adjunct for breakthrough control.
option_3_label: Switch to broad-spectrum monotherapy (VPA)
option_3_rationale: If carbamazepine is ineffective or not tolerated, valproate is an affordable broad-spectrum alternative that covers focal-to-bilateral tonic-clonic seizures in this 2-year-old male.

option_1_label: Optimize focal monotherapy with current anchor
option_1_rationale: CBZ is the correct first-line, affordable focal ASM here; without evidence of optimized dosing/adherence or documented failure, the best next step is to continue and optimize monotherapy.
option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct for breakthrough/prolonged seizures
option_2_rationale: If prolonged/breakthrough focal seizures persist despite adequate CBZ, clobazam is an accessible add-on that can reduce recurrence without changing the effective focal anchor.
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital if CBZ fails or is not tolerated/available
option_3_rationale: PB is widely available and effective for focal seizures in this setting and is a practical fallback when CBZ is ineffective, not tolerated, or cannot be reliably obtained.

option_1_rationale: Focal motor seizures are best matched to carbamazepine, which is the practical first-line anchor for focal epilepsy in Uganda and allows monotherapy optimization first.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for focal seizures, a reasonable alternative if carbamazepine is unavailable, poorly tolerated, or adherence/cost constraints dominate.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (alternative focal agent)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is an accessible focal-effective option if carbamazepine cannot be used, recognizing its interaction burden and need for careful dosing/monitoring where feasible.

option_1_rationale: Best-supported, affordable broad-spectrum choice for generalized convulsive seizures in this setting, appropriate for a child with high-risk prolonged seizure history.
option_2_rationale: Widely available and effective for generalized convulsive seizures in Uganda, a practical alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (broad-spectrum rapid-start alternative)
option_3_rationale: Broad-spectrum with rapid initiation (no slow titration like lamotrigine), reasonable if valproate/phenobarbital are unsuitable or supply/side effects dictate a switch.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, cost-effective first-line broad-spectrum choice for GTCS in this setting when there is no pregnancy or hepatic contraindication.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for generalized seizures in LMIC practice, suitable if valproate is not available or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (valproate-avoidance pathway)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is an alternative for GTCS if valproate must be avoided (e.g., approaching puberty/future teratogenicity concerns), acknowledging the need for slow titration.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is appropriate broad-spectrum coverage for multifocal seizures with spasms, and there is no documented failure at an adequate dose yet—optimize monotherapy before adding a second drug.
option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct for persistent spasms/jerks
option_2_rationale: If seizures/spasms persist despite adequate valproate dosing, clobazam is a practical, accessible adjunct that can improve control without the focal-worsening risks of CBZ/PHT.
option_3_label: Cost-anchored escalation using widely available agent
option_3_rationale: If control remains poor or clobazam is unavailable, phenobarbital is a highly available Uganda option for add-on therapy, acknowledging sedation/developmental trade-offs.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred broad-spectrum, cost-realistic first-line ASM for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in a 5-year-old when no contraindication is identified.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and affordable in Uganda and is an effective monotherapy option for GTCS if valproate is not feasible.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (accessible alternative)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin can control tonic-clonic seizures and is accessible, serving as a reasonable fallback if valproate and phenobarbital are unsuitable.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, broad-spectrum first-line choice for primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting when there is no pregnancy/hepatic contraindication.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and affordable in Uganda and is an acceptable GTCS monotherapy fallback when valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (alternative older GTCS option)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is a practical, accessible alternative for generalized tonic-clonic seizure control if valproate/phenobarbital cannot be used, acknowledging higher long-term adverse effect and interaction burden.

option_1_rationale: With seizure type not yet characterized and seizures frequent, valproate is the most practical broad-spectrum first-line option in this setting for generalized/mixed or uncertain epilepsy.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is inexpensive and widely available in Uganda and is a reasonable monotherapy fallback if valproate is not available or not tolerated, despite sedation/behavior risks.
option_3_label: Start rapid-titration broad-spectrum alternative (levetiracetam monotherapy)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam provides broad-spectrum coverage and can be started/titrated faster than lamotrigine, making it a good alternative when valproate or phenobarbital are unsuitable, while monitoring for behavioral adverse effects.

option_1_rationale: Seizure type is unclear and no adequate trial failure is documented; levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and is the safest hold while clarifying semiology and optimizing dose/adherence.
option_2_label: Switch to valproate monotherapy (LMIC broad-spectrum anchor)
option_2_rationale: If levetiracetam is ineffective, not tolerated, or unaffordable, valproate is the most cost-effective broad-spectrum option in Uganda for many non-convulsive/generalized epilepsies in this age group.
option_3_label: Absence-focused switch (only if typical absence is strongly suspected/confirmed)
option_3_rationale: If events are frequent brief staring spells with impaired awareness and no GTCS, ethosuximide is a targeted, effective option and avoids the seizure-worsening risk of CBZ/PHT in absence epilepsy.

option_1_rationale: Clear focal motor seizures in a 7-year-old with prolonged events—carbamazepine is the most appropriate cost-sensitive first-line focal ASM in this setting.
option_2_label: Use phenobarbital as the accessible fallback monotherapy
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is unavailable or not tolerated, phenobarbital is a widely available, effective focal-seizure option in Uganda despite sedation concerns.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (alternative focal agent)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is an accessible focal-effective alternative if both carbamazepine and phenobarbital are unsuitable, recognizing its higher long-term adverse-effect and interaction burden.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, broad-spectrum, and cost-practical first-line option for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this 7-year-old in Uganda, with no stated contraindications.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and affordable for GTCS and is a reasonable alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated, despite sedation/learning concerns.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (valproate-avoidance strategy)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is a broad-spectrum option with relatively favorable cognitive profile and can be used if valproate is contraindicated or must be avoided, recognizing it requires slow titration.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective and practical first-line option for frequent GTCS in this setting, with broad-spectrum coverage and good evidence.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly available and effective for GTCS in Uganda and is a reasonable alternative when valproate is not tolerated, unavailable, or unaffordable, acknowledging sedation risk.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (non–enzyme inducer alternative)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum option for GTCS and can be considered if valproate is contraindicated or declined, though cost and behavioral side effects may limit use.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate accessible broad-spectrum anchor for atonic/drop seizures in this setting and should be optimized as monotherapy first.
option_2_label: Start topiramate monotherapy (if valproate not suitable/available)
option_2_rationale: Topiramate is a broad-spectrum alternative for atonic seizures if valproate cannot be used, recognizing cognitive/weight side-effect risks.
option_3_label: Start valproate + clobazam (early adjunct if drop attacks/prolonged seizures persist)
option_3_rationale: If rapid additional suppression is needed due to ongoing prolonged/injurious events while valproate is being titrated, clobazam is an accessible adjunct option.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the default, cost-effective first-line anchor for focal seizures in this Uganda setting, and monotherapy should be optimized before adding a second drug.
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is unavailable or not tolerated, phenobarbital is a widely accessible focal-effective option in LMIC practice despite sedation risk.
option_3_label: Fallback focal monotherapy (PHT)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is an alternative accessible focal agent if CBZ and PB are not suitable, recognizing its higher interaction/toxicity burden for long-term use.

option_1_rationale: Broad-spectrum fit for generalized seizures and preferred over valproate in a 12-year-old female of childbearing potential, assuming seizures are not clearly myoclonic/absence-predominant needing valproate.
option_2_rationale: Highly available and effective for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting, used as a practical first-line when cost/availability or slow lamotrigine titration is a barrier.
option_3_label: Start valproate monotherapy (highest efficacy for generalized, but penalized)
option_3_rationale: Strongest broad-spectrum efficacy for generalized epilepsies in Uganda, but should be avoided if possible in females of childbearing potential unless alternatives are ineffective/unavailable and risks are explicitly addressed.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the first-line, cost-effective focal-seizure drug in this setting and there is no documented adequate-dose failure yet—optimize adherence and dose before changing therapy.
option_2_rationale: If school decline/handwriting issues suggest carbamazepine intolerance or if seizures continue despite adequate dosing, lamotrigine is an effective focal option with a generally better cognitive profile (but requires slow titration).
option_3_label: Switch to levetiracetam monotherapy (rapid-initiation alternative)
option_3_rationale: If carbamazepine is ineffective or not tolerated and a faster-onset regimen is needed than lamotrigine titration allows, levetiracetam is a broad/focal-effective alternative (monitor for behavioral side effects).

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-matched, cost-effective first-line ASM for GTCS here; with unclear dosing/adherence history, monotherapy should be optimized before adding another drug.
option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct for persistent seizures

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred, affordable broad-spectrum first-line for GTCS in this setting and fits best for a child with possible mixed/generalized epilepsy risk.

option_1_label: Start broad-spectrum anchor for mixed drop attacks + GTCS
option_1_rationale: Mixed focal drop attacks with occasional generalized tonic-clonic seizures favors a broad-spectrum, affordable first-line agent in this setting, and monotherapy has not yet been optimized.

option_3_label: Lowest-cost widely available monotherapy fallback
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is accessible and effective for focal and generalized tonic-clonic seizures and is a pragmatic alternative if valproate/carbamazepine are unavailable or not tolerated, despite sedation concerns.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the correct first-line focal ASM in Uganda and she is already on it; with no documented adequate-dose failure, the best next step is to continue and optimize monotherapy (dose/adherence) rather than add drugs.
option_2_label: Switch to levetiracetam monotherapy (if carbamazepine ineffective or not tolerated)

option_1_label: Start valproate monotherapy (LMIC first-line for generalized tonic seizures)
option_1_rationale: Best seizure-type match and the default affordable broad-spectrum anchor for generalized tonic seizures in this setting; prioritize monotherapy and titrate to effect.
option_2_rationale: Highly available and effective for generalized seizures in Uganda; reasonable if cost/supply constraints limit valproate use, with counseling about sedation.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (if VPA/PB unsuitable)

option_1_rationale: The child is currently seizure-free on carbamazepine with no documented adverse effects or failure, so continuing monotherapy is the most appropriate and conservative plan.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred, broad-spectrum first-line ASM for generalized convulsive seizures in this setting, and there is no clear evidence that optimized monotherapy has failed.
option_2_label: Add clobazam as adjunct to valproate (if breakthrough continues after optimization)

option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (cost/availability or intolerance pathway)
option_3_rationale: If valproate cannot be continued (e.g., intolerance, supply issues), phenobarbital is a widely available, effective low-cost alternative for generalized convulsive seizures, acknowledging sedation/developmental trade-offs.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the Uganda-appropriate first-line anchor for focal impaired-awareness seizures and should be optimized as monotherapy before considering add-on therapy.
option_2_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad/focal-effective and can be started quickly (useful when seizures are prolonged), serving as an alternative if carbamazepine is not tolerated, contraindicated, or unavailable.
option_3_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (lowest-cost fallback)
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly accessible and effective for focal seizures in resource-limited settings, but should be a fallback given sedation and potential worsening of school performance.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred, affordable broad-spectrum anchor for generalized seizure types in this setting and is an appropriate first monotherapy trial before adding a second drug.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly accessible and effective for generalized seizures in infants, and is a practical alternative if valproate is not feasible or if hepatic risk/monitoring concerns drive avoidance.
option_3_label: Start valproate and add clobazam (early escalation for very high daily burden)

option_1_rationale: Multifocal seizures most closely align with focal epilepsy; carbamazepine is the cost-effective first-line focal agent in this setting and should be optimized as monotherapy before escalation.
option_2_label: Start highly accessible infant-friendly monotherapy (PB baseline)

option_1_label: Start valproate monotherapy (LMIC first-line for GTCS)

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-matched, cost-effective first-line ASM for generalized convulsive seizures here, and failure at an adequate dose/adherence is not yet documented.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, practical broad-spectrum first-line option for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting when no contraindication is identified.

option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (non–enzyme-inducing alternative)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam provides broad-spectrum GTCS coverage and is an option if valproate/phenobarbital are not tolerated or unavailable, with close monitoring for irritability/behavioral worsening in ASD.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most practical broad-spectrum anchor available for epileptic spasms in this setting, and monotherapy should be confirmed optimized (dose/adherence) before adding a second ASM.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective and practical first-line ASM for primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting, without the behavioral-risk concern seen with levetiracetam.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly accessible and effective for GTCS in Uganda and is a reasonable alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated, acknowledging sedation/behavioral risks.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine matches focal/multifocal motor seizures and is the Uganda first-line anchor; with unclear dosing/adherence, monotherapy should be optimized before adding a second ASM.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, broad-spectrum, cost-appropriate first-line maintenance ASM for primary GTCS in this setting.

option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (valproate-avoidance pathway)

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, affordable broad-spectrum first-line option for generalized tonic-convulsive seizures in this 3-year-old with no clear contraindications.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the correct broad-spectrum first-line anchor for generalized convulsive seizures here, and monotherapy should be genuinely optimized (dose/adherence) before adding or switching.

option_3_label: Low-cost alternative broad-spectrum monotherapy if valproate not feasible
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for generalized convulsive seizures, and is a practical fallback if valproate cannot be used (e.g., intolerance, access), despite sedation concerns in a developmentally delayed child.

option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly accessible in Uganda and can substitute as an adjunct when levetiracetam is not feasible, though sedation/developmental effects must be monitored.

option_1_rationale: She is seizure-free since starting carbamazepine and monotherapy has not yet had time to be properly assessed; avoid unnecessary switching or polytherapy.

option_3_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (non-valproate broad option)
option_3_rationale: If valproate is not suitable and a broad-spectrum agent is preferred for generalized epilepsy, lamotrigine is a reasonable alternative (with the caveat of slow titration and rash risk).

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, affordable first-line ASM for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in a 4-year-old boy in this setting, with no stated contraindications.
option_2_rationale: If valproate is unavailable or not tolerated, phenobarbital is a widely available, low-cost monotherapy option for GTCS in Uganda.

option_1_label: Start/maintain valproate monotherapy (only if ongoing epilepsy needs treatment)

option_2_label: Phenobarbital monotherapy (lower-cost alternative when VPA not feasible)

option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is compatible with generalized convulsive seizures and may be preferable if phenobarbital sedation or valproate adverse effects are problematic, but requires slow titration and may be less accessible/costlier.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, cost-realistic first-line anchor for generalized convulsive seizures in this setting, whereas carbamazepine is not preferred for generalized epilepsy.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for convulsive seizures and is a reasonable fallback if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated, despite sedation risk in a child with developmental delay.

option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine was previously not tolerated or clearly ineffective, phenobarbital is a widely available, low-cost alternative for focal seizures (with counseling about sedation/behavioral effects).

option_3_rationale: If both carbamazepine and phenobarbital are unsuitable, lamotrigine is an effective focal option with a relatively favorable cognitive profile, acknowledging it requires slow titration for rash safety.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is first-line for generalized convulsive seizures in this setting and she is currently seizure-free on it, so the best strategy is to continue and optimize adherence/dose as needed.
option_2_label: Switch to phenobarbital if valproate cannot be used/obtained

option_1_label: Optimize focal monotherapy with current anchor
option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is first-line for focal seizures in this setting and there is no documented adequate-dose failure, so the priority is to continue and optimize monotherapy (dose/adherence) before adding another ASM.

option_2_label: Optimize/continue phenobarbital monotherapy (if dose/adherence not yet optimized)
option_2_rationale: If phenobarbital has not been adequately titrated or adherence is uncertain, optimizing the current accessible monotherapy is a reasonable, cost-sensitive strategy before switching.
option_3_label: Valproate plus clobazam (if seizures remain frequent during/after VPA optimization)
option_3_rationale: If monotherapy is clearly insufficient given ongoing frequent GTCS, adding an affordable adjunct (clobazam) to a valproate backbone can improve control while avoiding long-term phenobarbital-related sedation.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate first-line, affordable broad-spectrum choice for frequent generalized convulsive seizures in this setting, especially with very prolonged events.
option_2_rationale: If valproate is unavailable or not tolerated/contraindicated, phenobarbital is a widely available, effective option for generalized convulsive seizures in Uganda.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (fallback option)

option_2_rationale: If phenobarbital is ineffective at an adequate dose or poorly tolerated, valproate is the strongest accessible broad-spectrum monotherapy for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting.
option_3_label: Add valproate as adjunct to phenobarbital
option_3_rationale: If phenobarbital has been optimized yet seizures persist and immediate improved control is needed, adding valproate provides broad-spectrum coverage while maintaining the existing regimen (with close monitoring for sedation and hepatic adverse effects).

option_1_rationale: Focal semiology makes carbamazepine the most appropriate, affordable first-line anchor in Uganda, and prior exposure has no documented contraindication or failure.
option_2_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (if carbamazepine previously failed or was not tolerated)

option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for focal seizures in resource-limited settings, and is a practical fallback if carbamazepine/lamotrigine are not feasible despite sedation risk.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, cost-effective broad-spectrum first-line option for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting, and monotherapy has not yet been tried.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly available and affordable in Uganda and is an effective GTCS option if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated, despite sedation/learning concerns.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (cognition-friendly alternative)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is a broad-spectrum alternative that is generally favorable for cognition/learning, but requires slow titration and is typically used when valproate is not suitable or fails.

option_1_label: Start topiramate monotherapy (pragmatic alternative for spasms)
option_1_rationale: Infantile spasms lack their standard first-line agents in the available formulary, and topiramate is a reasonable alternative ASM to try as initial monotherapy with close early reassessment.
option_2_label: Start valproate monotherapy (broad-spectrum, cost-effective—use with strong infant hepatic caution)

option_3_label: Start clonazepam (bridge/adjunct-style choice when rapid symptomatic control is needed)
option_3_rationale: Clonazepam can reduce spasm clusters relatively quickly and is a pragmatic option if immediate short-term suppression is needed, recognizing sedation/respiratory risk in infants.

option_1_label: Start carbamazepine monotherapy (focal-first, least sedating baseline)

option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct for breakthrough/prolonged events (if CBZ optimized and still uncontrolled)
option_2_rationale: If seizures persist despite adequate CBZ, adding clobazam can improve control quickly without switching off the appropriate focal anchor, though sedation must be monitored.
option_3_label: Switch to an alternative low-cost focal antiseizure medicine (if CBZ ineffective or not tolerated)
option_3_rationale: If CBZ cannot be optimized (ineffective at reasonable dose, intolerable, or unavailable), phenytoin is an accessible focal-seizure option in Uganda, acknowledging interaction and long-term adverse-effect burdens.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, broad-spectrum, cost-practical first-line option for GTCS in this setting and is not contraindicated by the provided history.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly accessible and effective for GTCS in Uganda, and is a reasonable alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated (with counseling about sedation/behavior).
option_3_label: Start carbamazepine monotherapy (alternative if above not feasible)
option_3_rationale: Carbamazepine can control generalized tonic-clonic seizures and is affordable, serving as a practical alternative when valproate/phenobarbital cannot be used, though it is a narrower-spectrum choice.

option_1_rationale: Best match for focal motor seizures with secondary generalization and is the practical first-line anchor in Uganda before considering polytherapy.
option_2_rationale: A highly accessible, effective option for focal/GTCS if carbamazepine is unavailable or not tolerated, accepting sedation risk in a child with developmental delay.

option_3_rationale: Another practical focal-effective older ASM to use if carbamazepine cannot be used, though with higher long-term adverse-effect and interaction burden.

option_2_rationale: If seizures persist despite adequate valproate dosing, clobazam is a practical adjunct that can help clustered/spasm-like events without relying on focal-only agents.

option_1_rationale: If ongoing epilepsy treatment is indicated (i.e., seizures have recurred or risk is high), valproate is the best-matched, cost-effective broad-spectrum ASM for generalized convulsive seizures in this setting.
option_2_label: Conservative—no ASM for a single remote seizure
option_2_rationale: With only one generalized convulsive seizure reported ~1 year ago and no status/prolonged seizures, a reasonable approach is no maintenance ASM (and avoid restarting carbamazepine, which is not a preferred anchor for primary generalized seizures).
option_3_label: Low-cost alternative monotherapy
option_3_rationale: If an ASM must be started but valproate is unavailable/contraindicated, phenobarbital is a widely available, affordable monotherapy option, used cautiously given developmental delay and sedation risk.

option_1_label: Optimize current broad-spectrum dual therapy (no new drugs yet)
option_1_rationale: Both drugs are seizure-type compatible and already in use; with missing dose/response details, the safest first step is to optimize/verify adequacy before adding a third agent.
option_2_label: Add clobazam as affordable adjunct for persistent frequent tonic seizures
option_2_rationale: If seizures persist despite adequate VPA/LEV dosing, clobazam is a practical, accessible add-on that can reduce frequent brief generalized tonic seizures.
option_3_label: Phenobarbital-based escalation if control remains poor or clobazam not feasible
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and can improve control in refractory pediatric generalized seizures, but sedation/cognitive effects must be weighed carefully in CP/developmental delay.

option_1_label: Continue valproate, add clobazam for nocturnal clusters
option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best anchor for generalized + myoclonic seizures, and clobazam is a pragmatic, accessible add-on to rapidly reduce frequent nocturnal seizure clusters while valproate dosing/adherence is optimized.
option_2_rationale: If valproate has not yet been titrated to an adequate dose or adherence is uncertain, the first step is true monotherapy optimization before committing to long-term polytherapy.
option_3_label: Continue valproate, add levetiracetam
option_3_rationale: If seizures persist despite adequate valproate or if benzodiazepines are not suitable/available, levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum adjunct compatible with myoclonic features and can be initiated relatively quickly.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the best-fit, cost-effective first-line for focal seizures in this setting, and there is no clear evidence yet that optimized monotherapy has failed.
option_2_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (if carbamazepine ineffective or not tolerated)
option_2_rationale: Lamotrigine is an effective focal-seizure alternative with better long-term tolerability for some children, appropriate if carbamazepine cannot be optimized or is not tolerated.
option_3_label: Switch to phenytoin monotherapy (fallback if CBZ/LTG not feasible)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is an accessible focal-seizure option when carbamazepine cannot be used and lamotrigine is not feasible (e.g., availability or titration constraints), though long-term adverse effects/interactions are a concern.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-matched first-line ASM for GTCS in this setting/age, and monotherapy should be optimized first; clonazepam is a poor long-term baseline agent and may add sedation/tolerance.
option_2_label: Add clobazam as adjunct if seizures persist after valproate optimization
option_2_rationale: If verified adherence and adequate valproate dosing still leaves frequent seizures, clobazam is an accessible adjunct for breakthrough generalized seizures and is a more standard adjunct choice than chronic clonazepam.
option_3_label: Add phenobarbital as pragmatic LMIC adjunct if refractory or if clobazam not available
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and low-cost in Uganda and can be an effective add-on for persistent GTCS when valproate alone is insufficient, with sedation/behavioral effects monitored closely.

option_1_rationale: Suspected infantile spasms/West syndrome is poorly matched to carbamazepine; valproate is the most appropriate accessible broad-spectrum anchor within the allowed ASMs, prioritizing monotherapy first.
option_2_label: Valproate plus clobazam (rapid adjunct for very frequent/prolonged seizures)
option_2_rationale: With multiple daily prolonged seizures, adding clobazam can provide faster symptomatic control while valproate is established as the ongoing broad-spectrum backbone.
option_3_label: Topiramate-based strategy (if valproate not tolerated/ineffective)
option_3_rationale: If valproate is contraindicated or fails, topiramate is a broad-spectrum alternative within the formulary that is sometimes helpful in epileptic encephalopathy phenotypes, though cognitive/appetite effects must be watched closely.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, affordable broad-spectrum choice for generalized clonic seizures in this setting and supports monotherapy for a low monthly seizure frequency.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for generalized seizures and is a practical first-line alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (valproate/PB not suitable)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is a broad-spectrum option for generalized seizures when valproate or phenobarbital cannot be used, but requires slow titration and careful rash monitoring.

option_1_rationale: With seizure type unspecified, valproate is the most practical broad-spectrum first-line option in this setting and avoids the risk of worsening generalized seizure types seen with carbamazepine/phenytoin.
option_2_rationale: Lamotrigine is a reasonable broad-spectrum alternative if valproate is undesirable, but it requires slow titration and thus is less suitable if rapid control is needed.
option_3_label: Broad-spectrum with rapid start
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam can be started quickly and covers multiple seizure types, making it a practical alternative when rapid titration is important or if valproate cannot be used.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the default first-line for focal seizures in this setting; stopping valproate reduces unnecessary polytherapy while carbamazepine is optimized for seizure control.
option_2_label: Broad-spectrum monotherapy if CBZ not tolerated/ineffective
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine causes adverse effects, interactions, or fails after adequate dosing, valproate is an affordable broad-spectrum alternative that can still control focal seizures.
option_3_label: Accessible focal alternative monotherapy
option_3_rationale: If both current agents are not viable (tolerability, adherence, supply), phenytoin is a cost-effective focal-seizure option in Uganda, suitable as a fallback monotherapy.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best LMIC-first-line broad-spectrum maintenance ASM for generalized convulsive seizures, and monotherapy should be optimized before adding a second drug given unclear evidence of adequate-dose failure.
option_2_label: Add phenobarbital adjunct to valproate (if ongoing prolonged/breakthrough seizures despite optimized VPA)
option_2_rationale: For high-risk generalized convulsive seizures with prolonged events, phenobarbital is a highly accessible, effective adjunct in Uganda when valproate alone is insufficient.
option_3_label: Add clobazam adjunct to valproate (alternative add-on if PB not tolerated/available)
option_3_rationale: Clobazam is a broad-spectrum adjunct option that can reduce breakthrough seizures/prolonged episodes when valproate monotherapy is inadequate and phenobarbital is undesirable due to sedation or other concerns.

option_1_rationale: The child is seizure-free on carbamazepine and there is no documented mismatch seizure type or intolerance, so maintaining successful monotherapy is preferred.
option_2_rationale: If the diagnosis is confidently generalized epilepsy or new generalized seizure types emerge, valproate is the most appropriate affordable broad-spectrum anchor for GTCS in this setting.
option_3_label: Cost-access fallback monotherapy
option_3_rationale: If carbamazepine cannot be continued and valproate is unavailable/contraindicated, phenobarbital is an accessible alternative for GTCS in Uganda despite sedation risk.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the Uganda-appropriate first-line anchor for focal seizures and should be optimized as monotherapy before considering add-ons.
option_2_label: Use broad-spectrum alternative if focal diagnosis uncertain or CBZ unsuitable
option_2_rationale: Valproate is an accessible broad-spectrum option that covers focal-to-bilateral convulsions if seizure classification is uncertain or carbamazepine is not tolerated/available.
option_3_label: Low-cost fallback monotherapy if CBZ/VPA cannot be used
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly available and effective for convulsive seizures in LMIC settings, and can be used as a pragmatic alternative when other first choices are not feasible.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective and practical broad-spectrum first-line option for GTCS in this setting and is preferable to sedating alternatives in a developmentally vulnerable child.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is effective for GTCS and often the most accessible/affordable option in Uganda if valproate cannot be used.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (if valproate not suitable and PB poorly tolerated)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is an alternative broad-spectrum monotherapy for GTCS when valproate is avoided and phenobarbital sedation is problematic, acknowledging slower titration and higher cost/availability constraints.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective and practical first-line option for primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting, and monotherapy should be optimized first.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly accessible and effective for GTCS in Uganda and is a strong alternative if valproate is unavailable, unaffordable, or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (fallback)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is an accessible fallback ASM for convulsive seizures when first-line broad-spectrum options (valproate/phenobarbital) cannot be used, despite being less ideal for primary generalized epilepsy.

option_1_rationale: Seizure-free for 3 months on a broad-spectrum, LMIC-accessible agent with no stated contraindications—do not change a working regimen.
option_2_label: Switch to carbamazepine if seizures are confirmed focal-only and valproate is not desired/tolerated
option_2_rationale: If detailed semiology/EEG later supports purely focal epilepsy and there is a reason to avoid VPA, CBZ is the Uganda first-line focal anchor.
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital if valproate cannot be continued (e.g., supply/intolerance) and seizure type remains unclear
option_3_rationale: PB is widely available and affordable in this setting and provides broad antiseizure coverage as a pragmatic fallback when VPA cannot be used.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the first-line, broad-spectrum anchor for generalized tonic convulsive seizures here, and monotherapy should be optimized before adding another ASM.
option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct for breakthrough seizures
option_2_rationale: If seizures persist despite an adequate valproate dose/adherence, clobazam is a practical adjunct option with broad utility and rapid onset.
option_3_label: Reintroduce phenobarbital as a low-cost escalation path
option_3_rationale: If cost/availability limits other options or seizures remain uncontrolled after optimized valproate, phenobarbital is an accessible adjunct/alternative despite sedation and developmental side-effect concerns.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective and practical broad-spectrum first-line option for primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting, and monotherapy should be optimized first.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and affordable in Uganda and is an effective option for convulsive seizures when valproate is not feasible or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (alternative convulsive-seizure option)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin can control generalized convulsive seizures and is a reasonable low-cost alternative if valproate and phenobarbital are unsuitable or unavailable.

option_1_rationale: Best seizure-type match for focal epilepsy and the default cost-effective first-line anchor in this setting.
option_2_rationale: Highly available/affordable focal-effective option if carbamazepine is unavailable or not tolerated, acknowledging sedation risk in a toddler.
option_3_label: Start valproate monotherapy (broad-spectrum if focal diagnosis uncertain)
option_3_rationale: Reasonable alternative when there is concern for evolving mixed/generalized epilepsy or if carbamazepine is unsuitable, while remaining practical and effective.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective and practical broad-spectrum baseline choice for severe daily generalized seizures in this setting, provided pregnancy risk is not imminent and counseling is feasible.
option_2_rationale: If valproate should be avoided due to female reproductive risk concerns, lamotrigine is a reasonable broad-spectrum alternative for generalized seizures, recognizing slow titration.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (rapid-start alternative)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam can be initiated and escalated faster than lamotrigine for urgent seizure control, and is a useful alternative when valproate is avoided or not tolerated, with monitoring for behavioral side effects.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is first-line for focal motor seizures in this setting and the child is seizure-free, so maintaining effective monotherapy is preferred.
option_2_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (if carbamazepine not tolerated/contraindicated)
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine causes adverse effects or becomes unsuitable, lamotrigine is a compatible focal agent with a generally favorable neurodevelopmental tolerability profile (but requires slow titration).
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (cost/availability fallback)
option_3_rationale: If carbamazepine cannot be continued due to access or intolerance, phenobarbital is a widely available, low-cost alternative for focal seizures, acknowledging sedation risk in a developmentally impaired infant.

option_1_rationale: Focal onset with secondary generalization in an 11-year-old fits carbamazepine best as the Uganda cost-effective first-line anchor, and monotherapy should be optimized before adding another ASM.
option_2_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (accessible fallback if CBZ not feasible)
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for focal-to-bilateral tonic-clonic seizures, serving as a practical alternative if carbamazepine is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (alternative if CBZ intolerance/interaction concerns)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is effective for focal seizures and is a reasonable substitute when carbamazepine cannot be used, recognizing it requires slow titration due to rash risk.

option_1_rationale: Best seizure-type match (focal with impaired awareness) and the default cost-effective first-line option to initiate and titrate before considering polytherapy.
option_2_rationale: Reasonable if there is any concern the events are mixed/uncertain type or if carbamazepine is not tolerated/available, while remaining affordable and effective.
option_3_label: Access-focused fallback monotherapy
option_3_rationale: Highly available and effective for focal seizures in this setting, used when cost/availability limits other options, acknowledging higher sedation risk in a developmentally delayed child.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-fitting, cost-realistic first-line for primary generalized GTCS here, and carbamazepine is relatively mismatched for the stated seizure type.
option_2_rationale: Given ~3 GTCS/day, adding an accessible benzodiazepine adjunct can help achieve faster control while valproate is being established.
option_3_label: Phenobarbital monotherapy fallback
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for GTCS in this setting and is a reasonable alternative if valproate cannot be used.

option_1_label: Continue and optimize current broad-spectrum monotherapy
option_1_rationale: Valproate can control focal seizures and there is no documented adequate-dose failure yet, so optimizing/confirming adherence before switching is the most conservative first-visit approach.
option_2_rationale: Carbamazepine is the default first-line, cost-effective focal ASM in this setting and is preferred if focal seizures are continuing or valproate is not effective/tolerated.
option_3_label: Low-cost fallback focal monotherapy if CBZ not feasible
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for focal seizures in Uganda, and is a practical fallback when carbamazepine cannot be used or accessed despite sedation concerns.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best baseline first-line ASM for pediatric GTCS in this setting, and monotherapy should be genuinely optimized (dose/adherence) before adding a second drug.
option_2_label: Add clobazam to valproate (if seizures persist despite optimized VPA)
option_2_rationale: For ongoing prolonged/breakthrough GTCS after adequate valproate optimization, clobazam is a practical, broad-spectrum adjunct that can improve control without shifting away from the appropriate anchor drug.
option_3_label: Add phenobarbital to valproate (resource-limited fallback adjunct)
option_3_rationale: If control remains inadequate and/or clobazam is unavailable, phenobarbital is widely available and effective as an add-on in Uganda, accepting sedation risk given the high-risk prolonged seizures described.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective and cost-practical broad-spectrum anchor for frequent myoclonic seizures with occasional GTCS in this setting, and monotherapy has not yet been tried.
option_2_label: Valproate + clobazam (early adjunct if ongoing dangerous breakthroughs)
option_2_rationale: If seizure burden remains high or prolonged GTCS risk is felt to be immediate, adding clobazam provides accessible adjunct control while valproate is being established.
option_3_label: Levetiracetam monotherapy (if valproate not usable)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum alternative effective for myoclonic and GTCS, suitable if valproate is contraindicated, not tolerated, or unavailable.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is one of the most compatible and practical anchors available for this severe generalized epilepsy phenotype, and monotherapy should be confirmed optimized (dose/adherence/level) before adding a second ASM if optimization has not yet been done.
option_2_label: Add accessible rapid adjunct (benzodiazepine) to valproate
option_2_rationale: With ~3 spasms/day suggesting inadequate control, adding clobazam is a practical, fast-onset adjunct in Uganda that can reduce seizure burden while valproate is maintained.
option_3_label: Add broad-spectrum non-benzodiazepine adjunct to valproate
option_3_rationale: If clobazam is unavailable/not tolerated or sedation is problematic, levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum add-on that can be started quickly and may be better tolerated cognitively than topiramate or phenobarbital.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best first-line, broad-spectrum anchor for GTCS in this setting; before adding/switching, ensure adherence and that valproate has been titrated to an adequate/max tolerated dose given the very high seizure burden.
option_2_label: Add accessible rapid adjunct to valproate
option_2_rationale: If valproate has been optimized yet seizures persist (or rapid additional control is needed), clobazam is a practical, affordable add-on for breakthrough generalized convulsive seizures.
option_3_label: Add broad-spectrum non–enzyme-inducing adjunct
option_3_rationale: If clobazam is ineffective/not tolerated/unavailable, levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum alternative adjunct for uncontrolled GTCS, with minimal interaction burden compared with older enzyme inducers.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred, affordable broad-spectrum first-line ASM for GTCS in this setting and should be optimized as monotherapy first.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and inexpensive in Uganda and is a reasonable alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (alternative fallback)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is accessible and can control convulsive seizures, serving as a fallback if valproate and phenobarbital are unsuitable.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective and practical broad-spectrum first-line option for generalized tonic-convulsive seizures in this setting, appropriate for a 3-year-old without stated contraindications.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for generalized convulsive seizures and is a cost-sensitive alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (rapid-start alternative if high immediate risk or VPA/PB unsuitable)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and can be initiated quickly without slow titration, making it a reasonable alternative when rapid stabilization is prioritized or if valproate/phenobarbital are contraindicated or not feasible.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, affordable broad-spectrum anchor for generalized tonic seizures in this setting and is a strong first monotherapy choice.
option_2_rationale: Phenytoin can be effective for tonic seizures and can be initiated quickly if rapid seizure suppression is prioritized or if valproate is unavailable/contraindicated.
option_3_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (most accessible fallback)
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective across many seizure types, providing a practical low-cost option when other first-line choices cannot be used.

option_1_rationale: Seizure type is unclear but the child is seizure-free on this broad-spectrum combination, so the safest course is to continue without changes while clarifying seizure semiology and past response.
option_2_label: Cost-sensitive simplification to valproate monotherapy (planned step-down)
option_2_rationale: Valproate is the Uganda-appropriate anchor for generalized/mixed/uncertain epilepsy; if seizure freedom is stable and there is no documented monotherapy failure, stepping down from LEV reduces cost and polytherapy burden.
option_3_label: Alternative simplification to levetiracetam monotherapy (if valproate intolerance/contraindication emerges)
option_3_rationale: If valproate is not tolerated or hepatic risk becomes a concern, levetiracetam remains a broad-spectrum option that can maintain coverage without needing slow titration.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is first-line and well-matched for GTCS in this setting; with no documented adequate-dose failure, the priority is to continue and optimize monotherapy (confirm adherence/weight-based dosing) before adding another ASM.

option_3_label: Add phenobarbital as adjunct to valproate
option_3_rationale: If escalation is needed and cost/availability are key, phenobarbital is a widely available adjunct option in Uganda (with monitoring for sedation).

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective broad-spectrum, cost-reasonable first-line option for frequent generalized convulsive seizures in this setting when no contraindication is identified.
option_2_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (most accessible infant option)
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly available and effective for convulsive seizures in infants, a practical first-line choice if valproate is unavailable/contraindicated or rapid access is the priority.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (if older drugs not suitable)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and can be initiated quickly without slow titration, serving as a reasonable alternative if valproate/phenobarbital are not tolerated or cannot be used.

option_1_rationale: CBZ is the Uganda-appropriate, cost-effective first-line for focal seizures and is the best match to the described semiology if there was no true prior intolerance/failure.

option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (alternative focal agent)
option_3_rationale: PHT is another accessible focal-seizure option if CBZ is not suitable and PB sedation is problematic, recognizing its interaction burden and long-term adverse effects.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the default first-line, cost-effective option for focal motor seizures in this setting, with less chronic sedation than phenobarbital.

option_3_label: Alternative focal monotherapy (phenytoin)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is an effective, accessible focal-seizure option and a practical alternative if carbamazepine cannot be used and phenobarbital sedation is problematic.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the broad-spectrum, cost-effective anchor for this nonconvulsive status/Landau‑Kleffner context, and monotherapy should be optimized first since failure at adequate dose is not yet documented.

option_3_label: Low-cost broad-spectrum fallback if valproate not feasible
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and broad-coverage in resource-limited settings, and is a reasonable fallback if valproate is unavailable/contraindicated, despite sedation concerns.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred, cost-effective first-line for pediatric GTCS here; without clear evidence of optimized dosing failure, continue and optimize monotherapy before adding another drug.
option_2_rationale: If seizures are persisting despite adequate valproate dosing/adherence, clobazam is an accessible adjunct that can reduce breakthrough GTCS without narrowing seizure coverage.
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (resource-limited fallback)
option_3_rationale: If valproate is not tolerated, unavailable, or ineffective after true optimization, phenobarbital is a widely available, low-cost alternative for GTCS (with sedation monitoring).

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, practical first-line for GTCS here, and monotherapy failure at an adequate dose/adherence has not been documented.

option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and generally less sedating than phenobarbital, making it a useful alternative when developmental vulnerability makes sedative burden unacceptable and access/cost permit.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, broad-spectrum, cost-feasible first-line option for frequent generalized tonic-convulsive seizures in this setting when no contraindication is identified.
