option_1_rationale: Focal seizures with secondary generalization are best matched to carbamazepine, which is the practical first-line, cost-sensitive choice in this setting.
option_2_rationale: Valproate is a strong, affordable broad-spectrum option if there is any concern for mixed/generalized components or if carbamazepine is not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (access-first fallback)
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for convulsive seizures, and is a reasonable fallback if cost/availability limits carbamazepine/valproate, acknowledging sedation/developmental concerns.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, broad-spectrum, cost-anchored first-line option for frequent generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting when no contraindication is documented.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in Uganda and is a practical alternative if valproate is not feasible.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (broad-spectrum non-inducing)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and can be initiated without slow titration, making it a reasonable fallback if valproate/phenobarbital are contraindicated or not tolerated despite higher cost/availability concerns.

option_1_label: Start valproate monotherapy (LMIC first-line for generalized convulsions)
option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred broad-spectrum, cost-realistic anchor for generalized convulsive seizures in this setting and supports monotherapy for a child with very frequent seizures.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly available and effective for generalized convulsions and is a pragmatic alternative if valproate is unavailable, not tolerated, or contraindicated.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (if older agents unsuitable)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam provides broad-spectrum coverage and can be started quickly, making it a reasonable fallback if valproate/phenobarbital cannot be used, despite higher cost and possible behavioral side effects.

option_1_rationale: The patient is seizure-free on carbamazepine with no stated problems, so continuing monotherapy is the safest, most cost-effective approach.
option_2_rationale: If the diagnosis is clearly generalized epilepsy (or if absence/myoclonic seizures emerge), valproate is the most robust, affordable first-line option for GTCS in this setting.
option_3_label: Non-valproate broad-spectrum alternative
option_3_rationale: If valproate is not acceptable/tolerated and a broad-spectrum agent is needed for GTCS, lamotrigine is a reasonable alternative (with the practical caveat of slow titration).

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the Uganda first-line anchor for focal epilepsy, and there is no documented failure or intolerance—optimize adherence and dose before changing therapy.
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is ineffective, not tolerated, or unavailable, valproate is an affordable broad-spectrum alternative that can cover focal seizures.
option_3_label: Switch to phenytoin monotherapy (resource-limited fallback)
option_3_rationale: If neither carbamazepine nor valproate is suitable, phenytoin is a commonly available, focal-effective fallback in this setting despite higher interaction/long-term adverse-effect burden.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective broad-spectrum, cost-conscious first-line option for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting with no stated contraindications.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available/affordable and effective for GTCS, reasonable if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated (with counseling about sedation).
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (valproate-sparing alternative)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is an alternative broad-spectrum option if valproate is not suitable, but requires slow titration and may be less practical if rapid control is needed.

option_1_label: Start valproate monotherapy (preferred broad-spectrum in this formulary)
option_1_rationale: Within the allowed ASMs, valproate is the most practical broad-spectrum anchor in Uganda for an infantile epileptic syndrome, and monotherapy is preferred given current seizure freedom.
option_2_label: Start topiramate monotherapy (alternative if valproate not suitable)
option_2_rationale: Topiramate is a reasonable broad-spectrum alternative for epileptic spasms if valproate is not tolerated/contraindicated, while still aiming for monotherapy.
option_3_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (resource-limited fallback)
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and can maintain seizure control if other options are unavailable, though it is less targeted for spasms and more sedating.

option_1_rationale: Focal seizures are best matched to carbamazepine, and it is an affordable, standard first-line option in this setting.
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is not tolerated or interactions are a concern, lamotrigine is effective for focal epilepsy and is generally less sedating (with slow titration for rash risk).
option_3_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (access/cost fallback)
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly available and inexpensive in Uganda and is a reasonable fallback when access to other agents is limited, accepting sedation risk in a child with CP.

option_1_rationale: Generalized tonic seizures with prolonged events and no contraindications—valproate is the most practical broad-spectrum first-line anchor in this setting.
option_2_rationale: Lamotrigine is effective for tonic seizures and is a reasonable alternative if valproate is not tolerated or is contraindicated, acknowledging the need for slow titration.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (accessible tonic-focused fallback)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin can be effective for tonic seizures and is widely available/cost-sensitive, but is best reserved as a fallback due to interaction and chronic adverse-effect burden.

option_1_rationale: Valproate best matches GTCS + absence + drop attacks in this setting, and monotherapy should be genuinely optimized (dose/adherence) before escalating.
option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct for mixed generalized seizures
option_2_rationale: If valproate is already at an adequate dose and seizures persist daily, clobazam is a practical adjunct that can reduce breakthrough generalized seizures including drop attacks.
option_3_label: Add non-sedating broad-spectrum adjunct
option_3_rationale: If adjunct therapy is needed and sedation/cognitive concerns make benzodiazepines less suitable, levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum add-on that can be initiated quickly.

option_1_rationale: He is seizure-free on an appropriate first-line focal ASM in this setting, so continuing monotherapy is the safest and most cost-effective plan.
option_2_label: Switch to phenobarbital if CBZ not tolerated/available
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and affordable in Uganda and can control focal seizures if carbamazepine cannot be used, acknowledging sedation risk.
option_3_label: Switch to lamotrigine if cognitive/sedation concerns or CBZ adverse effects
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is effective for focal epilepsy and may be more favorable for cognition/alertness than PB/PHT, but requires slow titration and may be less practical/costlier.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-fitting, cost-effective first-line for GTCS here, and treatment failure is not yet established (dose/adherence not documented; last seizure 2 weeks ago).
option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct if breakthrough seizures persist
option_2_rationale: If GTCS continue despite optimized valproate, clobazam is a practical, accessible add-on that can reduce breakthrough seizures.
option_3_label: Broad-spectrum non-benzodiazepine adjunct strategy
option_3_rationale: If adjunct therapy is required and clobazam is unsuitable or ineffective, levetiracetam provides broad-spectrum coverage with minimal interaction burden.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the Uganda-appropriate first-line for focal epilepsy and there is no documented optimized-dose failure or intolerance, so continuing and titrating as needed is the most appropriate first step.
option_2_label: Switch to levetiracetam monotherapy if CBZ fails or is not tolerated
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is ineffective at an adequate dose or causes problematic adverse effects, levetiracetam is a broad/focal-effective alternative that can be initiated without slow titration.
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy as the low-cost/access fallback
option_3_rationale: If cost/availability or tolerability limits use of carbamazepine (and levetiracetam is not feasible), phenobarbital is an accessible focal-effective fallback in this setting, accepting sedation/developmental impact risks.

option_1_label: No ASM for now (no seizures documented)
option_1_rationale: With no seizures reported and no seizure type, there is no current indication to start an ASM; avoid exposing a developmentally delayed 2-year-old to unnecessary drug adverse effects.
option_2_label: Start valproate monotherapy if seizures are later confirmed but type remains unclear/generalized
option_2_rationale: If epilepsy is confirmed and the seizure type is uncertain or generalized, valproate is the practical broad-spectrum first-line anchor in this setting (male child, no pregnancy constraint).
option_3_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy if seizures are confirmed and valproate is not feasible
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and affordable in Uganda and can be used as a fallback monotherapy if treatment is needed but valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.

option_1_rationale: Best-fitting, affordable broad-spectrum choice for generalized convulsive seizures in this setting, appropriate to trial as initial optimized monotherapy.
option_2_rationale: Widely available and effective for generalized convulsions, a practical alternative if valproate is unavailable or contraindicated.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (non-inducing fallback)
option_3_rationale: Broad-spectrum option that can be started without slow titration and avoids enzyme induction, reserved for situations where valproate/phenobarbital are not suitable.

option_1_rationale: Patient is seizure-free on a seizure-type-appropriate, cost-effective broad-spectrum ASM; no clear reason to change or add therapy.
option_2_rationale: If seizures recur despite adequate valproate dosing/adherence, clobazam is an accessible adjunct for generalized convulsive seizures/status-risk contexts.
option_3_label: Non-sedating adjunct strategy—add levetiracetam to valproate
option_3_rationale: If escalation is needed and sedation/cognitive impact is a concern (developmental delay), levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum add-on option without slow titration.

option_1_rationale: Clear focal seizure semiology in a child with no prior ASM exposure—carbamazepine is the Uganda-appropriate first-line focal agent to start and titrate as monotherapy.
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is unavailable or not tolerated, phenobarbital is widely accessible and effective for focal seizures, with sedation risk to monitor.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (alternative focal agent)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is an effective, commonly available focal-seizure option if carbamazepine cannot be used, acknowledging higher interaction and long-term adverse-effect burden.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, affordable broad-spectrum first-line option for generalized convulsive seizures here, whereas carbamazepine is focal-leaning and not the preferred match for a generalized epilepsy presentation.
option_2_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (most accessible fallback)
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for generalized convulsive seizures and is a pragmatic alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Continue current carbamazepine while reassessing seizure classification/response (conservative hold)
option_3_rationale: If medication access or switching is difficult, continuing carbamazepine temporarily can be acceptable while confirming that events are truly generalized (vs focal-to-bilateral) and documenting response before changing therapy.

option_1_rationale: For generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this age group with no stated contraindications, valproate is the most effective and practical first-line option to optimize as monotherapy.
option_2_rationale: If valproate is unavailable, not tolerated, or there is a strong preference for the most accessible low-cost agent, phenobarbital is a reasonable monotherapy option despite sedation risk.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (broad-spectrum alternative if older drugs unsuitable)
option_3_rationale: If valproate and phenobarbital are not feasible (e.g., intolerance or access issues), levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum alternative that can be initiated without prolonged titration.

option_1_label: Continue valproate, add clobazam for breakthrough/prolonged seizures
option_1_rationale: Valproate is the correct generalized-seizure anchor and already in place; adding clobazam is a practical, rapid-onset adjunct in Uganda for escalating frequency and prolonged convulsions.
option_2_rationale: Because adequate-dose failure/adherence is not documented, the first conservative step can be to ensure valproate is truly optimized before committing to long-term polytherapy.
option_3_label: Continue valproate, add phenobarbital if clobazam unavailable/ineffective
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly accessible and effective in resource-limited settings for difficult generalized seizures, used as an add-on when seizure control is urgent despite sedation risk.

option_1_label: Continue/optimize carbamazepine while clarifying seizure type
option_1_rationale: With unknown seizure type and no documented CBZ failure, the safest first step is to continue and optimize current monotherapy while urgently characterizing seizures (CBZ is appropriate if events are focal/post-meningitic).
option_2_label: Switch to broad-spectrum valproate monotherapy (if seizure type unclear or generalized suspected)
option_2_rationale: If the semiology suggests generalized/absence/myoclonic or mixed seizures (where CBZ may worsen), valproate is the most practical broad-spectrum first-line option in this setting.
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (access-focused fallback)
option_3_rationale: If cost/availability are major barriers or adherence is difficult, phenobarbital is widely accessible and effective for many seizure types, acknowledging sedation risk in a child with developmental delay.

option_1_label: Continue and optimize current focal-leaning monotherapy while clarifying seizure type
option_1_rationale: Monotherapy has not been shown to have failed and CP commonly causes focal epilepsy; continue CBZ while urgently characterizing seizure type (and ensuring dosing/adherence) before changing therapy.
option_2_label: Switch to broad-spectrum first-line for generalized/mixed risk after convulsive status
option_2_rationale: Given generalized convulsive status epilepticus and uncertain future seizure type, valproate is the most practical broad-spectrum anchor in this setting if no hepatic contraindication.
option_3_label: Switch to highly accessible post-status alternative if valproate is unsuitable/unavailable
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available, effective after convulsive status, and is a pragmatic fallback when valproate cannot be used, accepting sedation/developmental side-effect tradeoffs.

option_1_label: Continue current effective monotherapy (with reassessment of syndrome)
option_1_rationale: The child is currently seizure-free and monotherapy has not clearly failed, so maintaining the effective, affordable regimen is preferred while confirming there are no absence/myoclonic features.
option_2_label: Switch to syndrome-appropriate first-line for idiopathic generalized epilepsy
option_2_rationale: If the diagnosis of idiopathic generalized epilepsy is confident (or absence/myoclonus is present), valproate is the most appropriate and practical broad-spectrum first-line option in this setting.
option_3_label: Switch to broad-spectrum alternative if valproate is not feasible
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is a broad-spectrum alternative for generalized seizures when valproate cannot be used, recognizing it requires slow titration and may be less ideal if myoclonic features exist.

option_1_rationale: The child is seizure-free and valproate is a cost-effective broad-spectrum option when seizure type is not documented.
option_2_label: Switch to levetiracetam monotherapy (if valproate intolerance/contraindication emerges)
option_2_rationale: LEV provides broad-spectrum coverage without the hepatic/teratogenic profile of valproate and is reasonable if VPA cannot be continued.
option_3_label: Switch to carbamazepine monotherapy (only if focal seizures are confirmed)
option_3_rationale: If history later clarifies a purely focal epilepsy syndrome, CBZ is the practical Uganda first-line focal agent, but it is avoided until seizure type is confirmed.

option_1_label: Switch to valproate monotherapy (preferred GTCS anchor)
option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-matched, cost-effective first-line ASM for GTCS in this setting, and CBZ is a suboptimal anchor for generalized epilepsy.
option_2_label: Rapid-control dual therapy while transitioning off CBZ
option_2_rationale: With very frequent GTCS (2/day), adding phenobarbital can provide practical, accessible additional seizure control while valproate is being established, with a plan to simplify later if control improves.
option_3_label: If valproate is not usable (e.g., prior clear intolerance or hepatic concern), use phenobarbital monotherapy
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for generalized seizures in LMIC settings and is a reasonable fallback if valproate cannot be used.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective broad-spectrum, practical first-line choice for GTCS in this setting and age group, with no contraindications provided.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and affordable in Uganda and is a reasonable monotherapy alternative if valproate is not tolerated or not available.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (alternative fallback)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin can control GTCS and is accessible, serving as a third-line option when valproate and phenobarbital are unsuitable.

option_1_rationale: Atonic drop attacks plus prior GTCS strongly favor a broad-spectrum agent; valproate is the most appropriate and practical first-line anchor in this setting.
option_2_label: Valproate + clobazam (if drop attacks persist or injury risk is high)
option_2_rationale: If valproate alone is unlikely to rapidly control very frequent drop attacks or if breakthrough continues after titration, clobazam is an accessible adjunct commonly effective for atonic seizures.
option_3_label: Avoid CBZ; use topiramate as broad-spectrum alternative if valproate cannot be used
option_3_rationale: When valproate is not tolerated/contraindicated, topiramate provides broad-spectrum coverage for generalized seizure types including drop attacks, avoiding sodium-channel–dominant choices like carbamazepine.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is first-line for focal seizures in this setting and the child is reported well-controlled, so maintain monotherapy rather than switching or adding drugs.
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is unavailable, not tolerated, or adherence is difficult, phenobarbital is a widely available, low-cost focal-effective alternative.
option_3_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (if CBZ not tolerated)
option_3_rationale: If carbamazepine causes adverse effects (e.g., rash/hyponatremia) or important interactions, lamotrigine is an effective focal option (with the practical caveat of slow titration).

option_1_rationale: CBZ is the best seizure-type match and the Uganda first-line focal anchor; with no documented adequate-dose failure, priority is to continue and optimize monotherapy (dose/adherence) before adding a second drug.
option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct after CBZ optimization
option_2_rationale: If CBZ is at an adequate/max tolerated dose and seizures persist, clobazam is a practical, accessible add-on for breakthrough focal-to-bilateral seizures.
option_3_label: Switch to broad-spectrum monotherapy if CBZ ineffective/intolerable
option_3_rationale: If CBZ clearly fails or is not tolerated, VPA is an affordable broad-spectrum alternative that can cover focal-to-bilateral seizures when classification or mixed features are a concern.

option_1_label: Optimize focal monotherapy with current anchor
option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is first-line for focal seizures in this setting and there is no documented adequate-dose failure yet, so monotherapy should be optimized before adding another ASM.
option_2_label: Add accessible adjunct for persistent frequent seizures
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine has been adequately titrated/adherence confirmed and seizures remain frequent, phenobarbital is a practical, affordable add-on for focal seizures (monitor sedation/development).
option_3_label: Switch to broad-spectrum alternative if CBZ ineffective or seizure-type uncertain
option_3_rationale: If carbamazepine is not tolerated/ineffective or if events are not purely focal (mixed types possible in CP), valproate is an affordable broad-spectrum alternative suitable for a male infant.

option_1_label: Start valproate monotherapy (first-line for generalized GTCS)
option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, affordable broad-spectrum option for primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting, and monotherapy has not yet been optimized.
option_2_rationale: If valproate is unavailable or not tolerated, phenobarbital is a widely available and effective low-cost alternative for generalized seizures, acknowledging sedation risk.
option_3_label: Escalate to dual therapy if valproate optimized but seizures persist
option_3_rationale: If prolonged/nighttime GTCS continue despite adequate valproate dosing, adding clobazam is a pragmatic, accessible adjunct approach without relying on newer higher-cost agents.

option_1_rationale: He is seizure-free for 2 months on valproate with no contraindications reported, so the safest, most cost-conscious approach is to continue without changes.
option_2_rationale: Carbamazepine is the LMIC default anchor for focal (mesial temporal) seizures and is a reasonable alternative if valproate becomes poorly tolerated, unavailable, or impractical.
option_3_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (if CBZ not suitable)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is focal-compatible and a reasonable substitute if carbamazepine is not tolerated or contraindicated, though it requires slow titration so it’s mainly for a planned, non-urgent switch.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the recommended first-line, affordable focal-seizure ASM in this setting and avoids the behavioral risk signal of levetiracetam in a child with ADHD/hyperactivity.
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is not tolerated/available or interactions are a concern, lamotrigine is effective for focal epilepsy and is often better aligned with developmental/attention vulnerability, though it requires slow titration.
option_3_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (access-focused fallback)
option_3_rationale: If cost/availability is the overriding constraint, phenobarbital is a widely available focal-seizure option in Uganda, acknowledging sedation/attention effects and the need to monitor school functioning.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the Uganda-appropriate first-line anchor for focal seizures, and seizures are infrequent with no documented adequate-dose failure—optimize/continue monotherapy before any escalation.
option_2_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy if carbamazepine fails or is not tolerated
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly available and effective for focal seizures in infants, serving as a practical fallback if carbamazepine is ineffective or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Switch to phenytoin monotherapy as an alternative accessible focal agent
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is another low-cost, focal-effective option if carbamazepine cannot be used and phenobarbital’s sedation is problematic or unacceptable.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best accessible broad-spectrum anchor within this limited formulary for spasms-like presentations, and monotherapy has not been clearly optimized yet (dose/adherence unknown).
option_2_rationale: If seizures persist despite an adequate/tolerated valproate dose, levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum add-on that can be started quickly and avoids sodium-channel–heavy combinations.
option_3_label: Add alternative adjunct (valproate + topiramate)
option_3_rationale: If levetiracetam is unavailable/ineffective and valproate is optimized, topiramate is a reasonable broad-spectrum adjunct option, with attention to appetite/weight and metabolic adverse effects.

option_1_rationale: No seizures are reported and seizure type is unknown, so the safest, most appropriate plan is to avoid starting an ASM until epileptic seizures are confirmed.
option_2_label: If generalized or mixed seizures are confirmed — start valproate monotherapy
option_2_rationale: If epilepsy is confirmed with generalized/mixed seizure types, valproate is the most effective broad-spectrum, affordable anchor in this setting.
option_3_label: If focal seizures are confirmed — start carbamazepine monotherapy
option_3_rationale: If epilepsy is confirmed with focal onset seizures, carbamazepine is the cost-effective first-line focal agent in Uganda.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, broad-spectrum and practical first-line option for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this male toddler in a cost-sensitive Uganda setting.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly accessible and effective for GTCS and is a reasonable alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated, with counseling/monitoring for sedation and behavior effects.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (if VPA/PB unsuitable)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and can be initiated without slow titration, but is placed third here because existing aggression may worsen on LEV and cost/availability may be limiting.

option_1_label: Continue and optimize current valproate monotherapy
option_1_rationale: Infantile spasms is high-risk but monotherapy failure is not documented; continuing (and ensuring an adequate/optimized dose) avoids premature polytherapy in a developmentally vulnerable infant.
option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct if spasms persist despite adequate valproate
option_2_rationale: If seizures are ongoing on optimized valproate, clobazam is a practical, accessible add-on option within this limited formulary.
option_3_label: Add a broad-spectrum non–enzyme-inducing adjunct if benzodiazepine not suitable/insufficient
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum alternative adjunct with low interaction burden and can be started without slow titration if rapid escalation is needed.

option_1_rationale: Most compatible and effective option in this formulary for likely atonic seizures causing drop attacks, and monotherapy is preferred at Visit_1.
option_2_rationale: If avoiding valproate due to female-of-childbearing-potential risk is prioritized, lamotrigine is a reasonable broad-spectrum alternative (noting slow titration and uncertain prior response details).
option_3_label: Valproate + clobazam (if rapid drop-attack suppression is needed)
option_3_rationale: For frequent weekly falls where faster short-term reduction is critical, adding clobazam can provide earlier symptomatic control while valproate is established, accepting sedation/learning trade-offs.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred, affordable broad-spectrum anchor for generalized convulsive seizures in this setting, and prior exposure has no documented failure or intolerance.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for generalized convulsive seizures in infants, and is a practical alternative if valproate is not feasible.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (non–enzyme inducer fallback)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and can be started quickly with fewer interaction issues, reserved for situations where valproate and phenobarbital are unsuitable or not tolerated.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, affordable broad-spectrum first-line choice for primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting and should be optimized as monotherapy first.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for GTCS and is a practical alternative if valproate is unavailable, not tolerated, or not feasible.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (fallback older option)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin can control generalized tonic-clonic seizures and may be used as a cost-accessible fallback when valproate and phenobarbital are unsuitable or not available.

option_1_rationale: Carbamazepine is the correct Uganda-appropriate first-line for focal seizures, and monotherapy has not been clearly documented as optimized or failed.
option_2_label: Add affordable adjunct for rapid seizure reduction (after confirming CBZ optimized/adherent)
option_2_rationale: If seizures continue despite adequate carbamazepine dosing/adherence, clobazam is a practical adjunct that can reduce high daily seizure burden without abandoning an appropriate anchor drug.
option_3_label: Switch to an accessible alternative focal monotherapy
option_3_rationale: If carbamazepine is ineffective or not tolerated after true optimization, phenobarbital is a widely available, low-cost alternative for focal epilepsy in this setting.

option_1_label: Start valproate monotherapy (GTCS-first in this setting)
option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective, broad-spectrum first-line option for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in a 6-year-old boy and is practical/affordable in Uganda.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for GTCS, and is a good alternative if valproate is unavailable or if there are concerns about liver function.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (fallback if others not feasible)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is an accessible option for GTCS when valproate/phenobarbital cannot be used, though it has a higher long-term adverse effect and interaction burden.

option_1_label: Start valproate monotherapy (Uganda baseline for generalized convulsive seizures)
option_1_rationale: Best-established broad-spectrum choice for generalized convulsive seizures in this setting, with no identified contraindication in this male child.
option_2_rationale: Highly available and affordable in Uganda and effective for generalized convulsive seizures, used when valproate is not feasible.
option_3_label: Start lamotrigine monotherapy (valproate-sparing alternative if supply/tolerability issues)
option_3_rationale: Broad-spectrum alternative for generalized tonic-clonic seizures with a relatively favorable cognitive profile, acknowledging the need for slow titration.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, affordable broad-spectrum first-line choice for primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures in this setting, and monotherapy should be optimized before adding a second drug.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly accessible and effective for generalized tonic-clonic seizures and is a pragmatic alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start phenytoin monotherapy (alternative tonic-clonic option)
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is an effective, commonly available option for tonic-clonic seizures and can be used if both valproate and phenobarbital are not feasible.

option_1_rationale: The child is seizure-free on carbamazepine with no documented contraindication or mixed seizure type suggesting harm, so maintaining effective monotherapy is preferred.
option_2_rationale: If carbamazepine is not tolerated, unavailable, or if the phenotype evolves toward mixed generalized seizures, valproate is the most practical first-line generalized option in this setting.
option_3_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (valproate-sparing alternative)
option_3_rationale: If valproate is undesirable and a broad-spectrum, cognitively gentle option is needed, lamotrigine is a reasonable alternative (with the caveat of slow titration and rash risk).

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most effective and practical broad-spectrum first-line option for GTCS in this setting, important given the history of prolonged (~10 min) seizures.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available and effective for tonic-clonic seizures and is a reasonable low-cost alternative if valproate is not feasible.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (avoid valproate; rapid start)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum, can be initiated without slow titration, and is a good alternative if avoiding valproate in a female child is prioritized and cost/availability permit.

option_1_rationale: Best seizure-type match and LMIC first-line for focal seizures; start and titrate to effect before adding a second drug.

option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (lowest-cost fallback)
option_3_rationale: If both CBZ and PHT are not feasible, PB is widely available and effective for focal seizures, with the tradeoff of more sedation/cognitive side effects.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is seizure-type compatible and there is no documented failure or intolerance; monotherapy should be genuinely optimized (dose/adherence) before switching or adding a second drug.
option_2_rationale: For confirmed focal (right parietal) epilepsy in this Uganda setting, carbamazepine is the preferred cost-effective anchor if valproate is ineffective or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Add a low-cost adjunct while keeping current ASM
option_3_rationale: If seizures are frequent/prolonged or persist despite an adequate valproate trial, adding clobazam is an accessible adjunct strategy while avoiding immediate multi-drug switching in a young child.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-matched, practical first-line ASM for GTCS here, and there is no clear evidence yet that optimized monotherapy has failed.
option_2_label: Add clobazam to valproate (if breakthrough seizures despite adequate VPA)
option_2_rationale: If GTCS persist despite an adequate valproate dose/adherence, clobazam is an accessible, cost-sensitive adjunct option.
option_3_label: Add levetiracetam to valproate (alternative adjunct strategy)
option_3_rationale: If ongoing seizures persist or clobazam is not suitable, levetiracetam provides broad-spectrum add-on coverage for GTCS without relying on enzyme induction.

option_1_rationale: Broad-spectrum first-line for generalized tonic convulsive seizures in this setting and a good default when ESES suggests possible mixed/generalized epilepsy.
option_2_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (alternative broad-spectrum, fast start)
option_2_rationale: Reasonable alternative if valproate is not tolerated/contraindicated or if a quickly initiated broad-spectrum option is preferred, with monitoring for behavioral worsening.
option_3_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (cost/availability fallback)
option_3_rationale: Highly accessible and effective for convulsive seizures in resource-limited settings, used when other options are unavailable or not feasible despite sedation/behavioral risks.

option_1_rationale: Best match for focal-onset seizures and the standard, cost-effective first-line anchor in Uganda when there is no prior ASM failure.
option_2_rationale: Broad-spectrum coverage for focal-to-bilateral GTCS and reasonable if carbamazepine is not tolerated, unavailable, or if seizure semiology is later found to be mixed/uncertain.
option_3_label: Start phenobarbital monotherapy (most accessible fallback)
option_3_rationale: Highly available and affordable option for focal seizures in resource-limited settings, used when cost/supply or adherence constraints limit other choices despite sedation concerns.

option_1_rationale: The child is seizure-free on valproate and monotherapy should be preserved unless there is a clear contraindication or intolerance.
option_2_rationale: If valproate needs to be avoided (tolerability/hepatic concerns/long-term risk discussion in a female), carbamazepine is the default focal epilepsy anchor in this setting.
option_3_label: Access-focused fallback monotherapy
option_3_rationale: If cost/availability limits use of valproate or carbamazepine, phenobarbital is a widely available focal-effective alternative, used as a pragmatic fallback despite sedation risk.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the most appropriate, cost-effective broad-spectrum first-line for frequent GTCS in a 3-year-old with no stated contraindications.
option_2_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly available and effective for generalized tonic-clonic seizures, and is a reasonable first-line alternative if valproate is unavailable or not tolerated.
option_3_label: Start levetiracetam monotherapy (if older drugs not suitable)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and can be initiated quickly; reserve for situations where valproate/phenobarbital are contraindicated, not tolerated, or inaccessible.

option_1_rationale: The child is seizure free on arrival and valproate is a reasonable broad-spectrum anchor in this resource-limited setting, so the priority is to maintain and optimize effective monotherapy.
option_2_label: Add accessible adjunct if spasms recur despite optimized valproate
option_2_rationale: If seizures return after ensuring valproate is at an adequate tolerated dose, clobazam is a practical, relatively accessible adjunct for additional suppression.
option_3_label: Escalate with broad-spectrum alternative if adjunct benzodiazepine is not effective/feasible
option_3_rationale: Topiramate is a broad-spectrum add-on option within the allowed list for refractory spasms, used cautiously given potential cognitive side effects in a child with developmental delay.
